subject: Internal medicine
title: Wound healing
preambleMarkdown:
# Wound healing

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Wound healing is a clinical condition or syndrome in internal medicine.
- This article focuses on adult presentation, differential diagnosis, diagnostics, complications, chronic management, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Wound healing to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Wound healing is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Wound healing depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In internal medicine, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Wound healing.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Wound healing disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Wound healing should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Wound healing first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Wound healing, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Wound healing, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused internal medicine examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Wound healing presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Wound healing with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Wound healing is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Wound healing should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Wound healing is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Wound healing, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Wound healing may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Wound healing patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Wound healing, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Wound healing in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Wound healing, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Wound healing does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Wound healing may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Wound healing, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Wound healing as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Wound healing draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

### Source Excerpt: Wounds and Scars

_Source lines: 14761-15155._

WOUNDS AND SCARS
I. Wounds
A. Phases of wound healing
l . Inflammation
a. Vascular response
( l ) Injured vessels allow leakage and edema formation.

(2) Vasoconstriction then reduces further blood loss.

(3) Platelets arrive and adhere to the exposed endothelial lining (collagen) .

They release growth factors and chemotactic agents such as vascular
endothelial growth factor, prostaglandin E2, platelet-derived growth
factor, transforming growth factor alpha (TGF-a) , thrombin, and
thromboxane A2.

(4) Vasodilatation then resumes and contributes to the pain, swelling, and
redness seen in most acute wounds.

keratinocytes

I n h i bits TN F, I L-1 , I L-6, i n h i bits macrophage
and PMN activation
P M N , polymorphonuclear l e ukocyte .

b. Cellular response (Table 1 2 - l )
( 1 ) Reduced blood flow through the area of injury forces the traveling
WBCs to be pushed against the sidewalls of the vessel (margination) .

These WBCs then migrate through the vessel walls and move toward
the zone of injury during the first 24 hours.

(a) The WBCs are guided by changes in pH, chemotaxins produced by
dead or dying cells , and bacterial toxins.

(b) This begins the process of clearing the wound of foreign material.

(2) Monocytes arrive next (2 to 3 days) and become macrophages in the
interstitium.

(a) These contribute to wound cleaning not only by direct
phagocytosis but also as the source of over 30 growth factors as
well as cytokines.

(b) Mast cells also contribute to the wound clearance at this point.

## 2 . Proliferation

Proliferative cytokines and inflammatory mediators are defined and described
in Table 1 2-2.

a. Typically begins within about 48 hours of injury
b. Has four major elements
( 1 ) Angiogenesis : Endothelial cells adjacent to the zone of injury bud and
grow into the wound.

(a) Led by ischemia and chemical mediators
(b) Form new capillary beds, thus providing nutrition and avenues for
removal of waste products
(c) Lymphatics also regenerate and aid in lessening the edema within
the wound.

(2) Granulation: temporary connective tissue that fills the wound
(a) Fibroblasts are important elements in the formation of the
extracellular matrix.

(b) Fibroblasts are drawn into the wound by ischemia.

(3) Contraction: Fibroblasts are transformed into myofibroblasts and are
capable of action similar to smooth muscle. MyoHbroblasts are actin
rich and stretch the dermis to attempt to cover the wound.

i n it i a l stre n g t h .

factor; M M Ps. matrix meta l l oprote i nases; PDGF. plate l et-derived growth factor; TGF-a. transforming growth factor alpha;
TGF-. transform ing growth factor beta; VEGF. vascular endothe l i a g rowth factor.

( 4) Epithelialization: Keratinocytes from the wound margins and append
ages multiply and migrate across the wound.

(a) Keratinocytes further debride the wound and require an oxygen
rich environment.

(b) Thus, if the wound is full of debris or has poor blood supply,
reepithelialization is hindered.

## 3. Maturation

a. Collagen within granulation tissue is further remodeled through a process
of creation and destruction via collagenases.

( l ) This process can take up to 2 years to complete but is primarily
complete by 6 to l2 months.

(2) The collagenases convert immature collagen (type III) to mature
collagen (type I) .

b. The collagen realigns itself through an interaction of forces, both internal
and external.

( l ) Internal alignment comes when the collagen attempts to match the
collagen found at the wound edges .

(2) External alignment is based on the forces applied to the wound.

B. Wound closure
l . Primary intention
a. Clean wounds in which the edges are approximated either by suture ,
staples, or other adhesives.

b . This limits the inflammatory and proliferative phases. In fact, epithelialization
begins in as early as 24 hours.

2. Secondary intention
a. The wounds are not approximated.

b . This requires all phases of healing and is therefore longer and requires more
energy.

c. This allows for contaminated wounds to be removed by the physiologic
(phagocytic) or mechanical means .

d. Open wounds are less prone to infection.

S K I N A N D S O F T T I S S U E e 205

## 3 . Tertiary intention: also known as a delayed primary closure

a. The wound is left open initially for a certain period of time while the
wound is cleaned and observed.

b . The wound edges are then approximated in the usual fashion with stitches
or adhesive.

C . Factors that affect wound healing
l . Cause of wound: Sharply incised wounds heal faster than blunt traumatic
wounds .

2. Time since injury: Delay in presentation and cleaning can prolong the inflam
matory phase and thus delay wound healing.

3. Location: Wounds that occur in areas with reduced vascular supply heal more
slowly.

4. Size: Wounds heal Xom the periphery; thus, the larger the area auected, the
longer it will take for the wound to progress through the phases of healing.

5. Temperature: Decreased temperature inhibits wound healing.

6. Hydration: Dry wounds progress through the phases of healing more slowly
than those in a moist environment.

7. Foreign bodies: prolong the inflammatory phase and delay wound healing
8. Necrotic tissue: impairs wound healing by providing a medium for microbes
to exist
9. Irradiation: causes endothelial cell damage and hypoxia
a. Inhibits angiogenesis
b. Impairs keratinocytes and Hbroblasts in their G2-phase and S-phase of
replication

## 1 0 . Diabetes: impaired microcirculation and red cell function, resulting in poor

blood flow to the wound
a. Decreased leukocyte and lymphocyte function
b. Increased collagen degradation and poor collagen reformation

## 1 1 . Infection: prolongs inflammation and slows healing overall

a. For a wound to be considered infected, the quantitative amount of bacteria
is greater than 1 05 microbes per gram of tissue .

b . Amounts less than this imply colonization and may still impair wound
healing through their competition for oxygen within the wound.

1 2 . Age: As people age , their macrophage and fibroblast functions are reduced.

Cellular turnover is decreased, and microvasculature is reduced.

13. Nutrition: Adequate nutrition is vital to the healing process because all of the
phases of wound healing require energy to complete.

a. Vitamins A and C are especially important.

b. Zinc deficiency inhibits collagen formation and delays wound healing.

14. Smoking: Nicotine acts to constrict the microvasculature, thereby reducing the
blood flow within a wound.

a. Nicotine causes platelet aggregation and clot, thus impairing blood flow to
the wound.

b . Carbon monoxide binds to the red blood cells and thus limits the amount
of oxygen available to the wound bed.

## 1 5 . Medications that impair wound healing

a. Glucocorticosteroids
b. Doxorubicin
c. Tamoxifen
d. High-dose nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs
1 6 . Oxygen content: Polymorphonuclear neutrophils require Pa02 levels greater
than 50 mm Hg to produce superoxide derivatives.

a. Typically, oxygen levels at the center of a wound are significantly lower
than those in the surrounding tissue . In areas of hypoxia, collagen synthesis
is driven by glycolysis , but oxygen is essential for formation of the triple
helix and cross-linking.

b . The role of hyperbaric oxygen in wound healing is to increase the oxygen
levels in the bed of the wound and thus stimulate growth.

Vita m i n A acts to reverse
ste r o i d a l effects o n lyso
somes and can i m p rove
w o u n d h e a l i n g i n patie nts
who r e q u i re ste r o i d s .

D . Wound management
l . Description of the wound
a. Depth
( l ) Superficial (stage I)
(2) Violating the dermis (stage II)
(3) Violating the subcutaneous tissue (stage III)
( 4) Violating to the deep structures of muscle or bone (stage IV)
b . Dimension
c. Location
d. Presence of infection
( l ) Purulence
(2) Erythema
(3) Crepitus
( 4) Induration

## 2. Debridement

a. BeneHts
( l ) Reduces bacterial load of a wound
(2) Increases effectiveness of antibacterials applied to the wound
(3) Improves action of leukocytes: provides an aerobic environment for the
opsonization and phagocytosis of bacteria
( 4) Shortens the inflammatory phase of wound healing
(5) Decreases the energy required for wound healing
(6) Decreases the physical barrier to wound healing
b. Indications for debridement
( l ) Necrotic, devitalized, and dead tissue
(2) Foreign matter and debris
(3) Disorganized, violet, and dusky-colored granulation tissue
(4) Blisters, if they impede function or they contain pus (otherwise, they
can be considered sterile biologic dressings)
(5) Callus (a relative indication based on the clinical presentation of
the patient, e.g. , associated with a wound or present in the feet of a
diabetic patient)
c. Contraindications for debridement
( l ) Organized, uniform, and brightly pink granulation tissue
(2) Viable tissue
(3) Deep tissue injuries
(4) Healthy deep tissues (e.g. , muscle, tendon, ligament, nerve , capsule
of j oint)
d. Types of debridement
( l ) Sharp : removal of tissue with scalpel or scissors
(a) This is commonly performed at the bedside or in the operating room.

(b) This is the fastest means of debridement but is associated with pain
and other complications such as bleeding.

(2) Autolytic: removal of tissue by providing an environment for the body's
own defenses to work
(a) Application of a moisture-retentive dressing to allow for the body's
own collagenases and inflammatory cells to soften and debride
the wound
(b) Principles of topical therapy
i. Remove necrotic tissue
ii. Identify and eliminate infection
iii. Obliterate dead space
iv. Absorb excess exudates
v. Maintain moist environment
vi. Thermal insulation
vii. Protection from trauma
(c) Labor intensive and requires more time to clean the wound
(d) Lower rate of infection than with traditional gauze dressings

S K I N A N D S O F T T I S S U E e 2 0 7
(3) Enzymatic: application of enzymes topically to the wound. The U . S .

II. Scars
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has removed fibrinolytics and
proteolytics from the market for patient safety reasons , leaving collage
nase as the only approved debrider.

(a) Time consuming: Typical course is daily wound application for
2 weeks .

(b) Labor intensive and costly
( 4) Mechanical
(a) Wet-to-dry dressings
i. Inexpensive and work intensive . Such dressings should be
changed twice daily.

ii. Good for removing debris within the wound
iii. May traumatize the wound and remove viable tissues
iv. Do not maintain a moist environment.

v. Promotes granulation tissue formation
(b) Whirlpool
i. Nonspecific
ii. Good for removing debris within the wound
(c) Scrubbing
i. NonspeciHc
ii. Cytotoxic: Hydrogen peroxide and Betadine are especially toxic
to tissues and should be avoided in open wounds.

iii. May be painful and traumatic to tissues
A. Keloid
l . Uncommon, occurs mainly in dark-skinned patients
2. Extends beyond the boundary of the original scar and invades the surrounding
normal tissue
3. Genetic predisposition
4. Characterized by an overabundance of collagen. Collagen formation outpaces
the actions of collagenases.

5. Can be considered a benign neoplasm with the potential for recurrence

## 6. Treatment

a. Steroid injection
b. Re-excision followed by radiation
c. No single therapy with good results
B. Hypertrophic scars
l . Overabundance of collagen
2. Contained within the borders of the original scar

## 3. Treatment

a. Re-excision, especially for wounds allowed to close by secondary intention
b. Application of silicone sheets
c. Pressure garments
d. Steroid inj ections

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.
